title: Project Trash
subtitle: Designing the UX structure and UI architecture for a chaotic physics-based game experience.

intro.heading: How do you structure chaos?
intro.body:
Project Trash is a chaotic physics-based game where players throw trash objects into bins while unpredictable events unfold around them.

The challenge was creating a gameplay experience that felt playful and chaotic while still remaining readable and understandable for the player.

My work focused on structuring the gameplay UX and defining how the UI system should support the experience.

role.heading: The translator between design and development
role.body:
I worked on structuring the gameplay UX and helping translate design ideas into a clear UI architecture.

Working closely with the UI lead and the UI programmer, I helped define the menu structure, gameplay flow, and reusable UI prefabs so programmers could implement the interface more efficiently inside Unity.

problem.heading: Balancing chaos with clarity
problem.body:
The core gameplay is intentionally chaotic. Trash objects bounce around, physics interactions create unpredictable situations, and players must react quickly.

Without clear UX structure this kind of gameplay easily becomes confusing instead of fun. Players need to understand what is happening, what they should focus on, and how they are progressing.

The challenge therefore became structuring the gameplay experience so the chaos remained entertaining while the interaction stayed readable.

image.alt: Gameplay UX structure showing player motivations and feature mapping

approach.heading: Designing a UI system developers could build
approach.body:
To support the gameplay experience, the interface needed to be modular and easy for programmers to implement.

I helped define a set of reusable UI prefabs and menu structures that could be reused across different parts of the game. This structure allowed developers to build UI screens more efficiently while maintaining consistency across menus, overlays and gameplay elements.
bullets: Clear UI flow connecting menus, gameplay and feedback overlays
Reusable UI prefabs reduced implementation time for developers
Gameplay stayed readable despite intentional chaos

image.alt: Reusable UI prefabs and menu system structure

outcome.heading: A structured gameplay and UI flow
outcome.body:
The final result was a clear UI flow connecting menus, gameplay screens and feedback overlays.

By structuring the experience and defining reusable UI elements early, the team could implement the interface more efficiently while keeping the gameplay readable for players.

image.alt: UI flow showing gameplay screens and interaction structure

keyLearning.heading: UX structure supports development
keyLearning.body:
One of the most important lessons from this project was how much good UX structure helps development teams.

By defining interaction flows and UI architecture early, we reduced implementation friction and made it easier for programmers to translate the design into working systems.

cards.title: Structure first
cards.body: Defining the UI architecture and screen flow early gave developers a clear structure to build from, reducing guesswork during implementation.

cards.title: Design for your team
cards.body: By structuring reusable UI prefabs and menu layouts, the team could build new screens consistently without redesigning UI patterns each time.
